title: Case Study Day: Contracts, Client Alignment, and Business Systems
summary: Erin opened by sharing insights from a podcast about business efficiency frameworks — including the prioritize-improve-reduce method and the concept of white space between meetings. The group then dove into a wide-ranging discussion on contract strategies, handling clients who want to bring in their own subcontractors, and the importance of long-term client alignment over short-term revenue. A live role-play exercise demonstrated how to respond to a client pushing back on pricing and scope.

teachings.explanation: From efficiency expert Juliet Funt: first ask what's holding people up to get a baseline, then improve the identified process, and finally reduce it — ideally to six steps. This creates leaner, more effective systems in any business.
teachings.quote: Her goal is to reduce something to six steps, that's it. And I'm looking at our systems and I'm like oh my gosh we have a kabillion steps like we've got some reducing to do.

teachings.explanation: Studies show people spend only 14 minutes a day on what is most critical. Creating intentional gaps between meetings allows time to collect thoughts, generate solutions, and work through accumulated items — dramatically increasing daily effectiveness.
teachings.quote: When they get people's feedback from work at the end of the day on average people are only spending 14 minutes a day on what is most critical. 14 minutes.

teachings.concept: The Contract Is Not Your Protection — The System Is
teachings.explanation: A well-built delivery system provides more real-world protection than a contract. Contracts matter most for the rare bad-fit client, but obsessing over them can distract from the more important work of building reliable processes and selecting the right clients.
teachings.quote: I spent fifteen thousand dollars on a contract. I exited that contract. I was so busy trying to protect myself that I didn't recognize who I was going to end up working with.

teachings.concept: Time, Money, People — The Three Triggers for a Client Conversation
teachings.explanation: Any project situation that touches time, money, or people warrants an explicit conversation with the client. These three categories cover virtually every risk in construction and design, and workshopping them openly prevents misunderstandings and scope conflicts.
teachings.quote: Anything in my day that has to do with time, money or people is a conversation. If it hits any of those points with the client — time money people — we workshop that with our clients.

teachings.concept: Project Goals vs. Personal Framing in Client Conversations
teachings.explanation: When addressing scope conflicts or client pushback, framing issues around shared project goals rather than personal preferences de-escalates tension and creates alignment. Saying 'here are the project risks' lands better than 'I won't allow that.'
teachings.quote: The difference is — here are the project goals, this is what's required to achieve the project goals. When we diffuse that with people, then we're connecting.

teachings.explanation: To scale a business, you cannot be emotionally attached to the revenue or profit of any single project. Caring deeply about clients and outcomes is healthy, but attachment to the money of a specific deal leads to accepting misaligned clients and undermines long-term growth.
teachings.quote: In order to scale a business you can never be attached to the project emotionally for the people emotionally — never. I'm not attached to the money of the project.

teachings.concept: The Long View on Client Selection
teachings.explanation: The clients you take now directly shape the trajectory of your business. Saying no to misaligned clients, even at financial cost, positions you to attract better clients over time. Short-term pain from turning down revenue is often worth the long-term business health.
teachings.quote: There was two years where I said no because I knew the long view. And those clients — because of them we got into their worlds and that's just how it works. That long view is really hard. It's so hard to take. And if you're willing to believe in yourself enough to take it, it works.

teachings.explanation: When cash flow demands taking on a less-than-ideal client, do so consciously and communicate the 'why' to your team. Going in with clarity — knowing this is a survival move to bridge to better work — prevents team burnout and keeps everyone aligned.
teachings.quote: Going in with that mentality — yes at least because you're clear, it's controlled, it's clear, you understand the why, your team understands the why. You don't want people quitting on you while you're going through this.

actionable_advice: Use the AIA contract (remodel or new construction version) as a starting point rather than drafting from scratch — it saves significant time and is already 15 pages of solid coverage.
Hire an attorney to review and customize your contract to your specific business model and state laws rather than spending tens of thousands drafting one from scratch.
Add photography rights to your contract before the end of a project so clients cannot deny access to images for your portfolio.
Create white space gaps between meetings — use that time to update a running document of items to address with team members rather than pinging them throughout the day.
When a client wants to bring in their own subcontractor, paint a picture of all the ways it can go wrong — delays, warranty gaps, schedule disruption — rather than simply saying no.
Frame client conversations around shared project goals rather than personal preferences to de-escalate conflict and build alignment.
Evaluate every client-introduced subcontractor or vendor against the three criteria of time, money, and people risk before accepting or rejecting them.
When you must take a misaligned client for cash flow reasons, be transparent with your team about the situation so everyone understands the why and stays committed.
Only accept roughly 30% of projects that come across your table — be selective enough to build a client roster that reflects the business you want.
Listen to Jefferson Fisher's podcast for practical communication frameworks to help defuse tense client situations through language shifts.
client_challenges: Tiffany struggles with contracts being a bottleneck — she avoids starting projects because writing a custom contract for each one feels overwhelming and inefficient.

Charles operates in Manhattan with a vertically integrated business including an in-house millwork shop, and a client tried to remove the millwork scope and bring in a cheaper third-party vendor, disrupting schedule and quality.
Charles questions whether using his own custom contract instead of the industry-standard AIA contract makes him appear unqualified when bidding against larger competitors in Manhattan.

A participant is at a career stage where they are trying to cross the bridge from taking whatever work comes in to being selective — wrestling with when to say no versus when cash flow demands saying yes.
Charles realized his submissiveness in client conversations — not voicing strong opinions about product quality — led to clients making inferior choices that ultimately hurt the project.
Group members struggle with how to emotionally detach from the revenue of a project while still caring deeply about clients and outcomes.
Participants are unsure how to handle clients who cite personal skills (e.g., a retired mechanic who wants to install his own pocket doors) to justify reducing scope and cost.
notable_quotes: On contracts vs. systems: 'I spent fifteen thousand dollars on a contract. I exited that contract. I was so busy trying to protect myself that I didn't recognize who I was going to end up working with.'
On the contract's true purpose: 'The piece of paperwork — the contract — you burn it. It's not even important. The bigger thing is to align with the right clients.'
On client conversations: 'Anything in my day that has to do with time, money or people is a conversation. Time money people — like we workshop that with our clients.'

On non-attachment to revenue: 'In order to scale a business you can never be attached to the project emotionally — never. I'm not attached to the money of the project. I care deeply, deeply, deeply, deeply — like I'm godparents of people we've built for their kids.'
On the long view: 'There was two years where I said no. I have five kids. That was a risk. And it worked. If you're willing to believe in yourself enough to take it, it works.'

On pocket doors during role-play: 'When I'm done building your home I love you and I'm gonna have coffee with you — but I'm not coming back to deal with pocket doors. Everything I touch, I own. This is what I can stand by.'
